ECON 470 Homework 3.3¶

Claire Hamilton¶

Summarize the Data¶

Question 1¶

A tibble: 6 × 5
yearMean Star RatingMean EnrollmentMean Market SharePlan Count
<dbl><dbl><dbl><dbl><int>
20102.97346.990.064859579
20113.26388.310.084455249
20123.36410.650.090358169
20133.55406.730.082864174
20143.81451.110.082058636
20153.96482.570.081960819

Star ratings, mean enrollments, and mean market shares tend to increase over time.

Question 2¶

A tibble: 6 × 4
yearMean EnrollmentMean Market SharePlan Count
<dbl><dbl><dbl><int>
2010107.340.067348643
2011167.900.090012754
2012172.730.0567 9077
2013213.500.0600 3661
2014257.600.0557 3708
2015314.210.0405 4680

The plans without a star rating tend to have lower average enrollments and market share compared to plans with a star rating.

Question 3¶

The distribution of star ratings has shifted to the right over time, meaning plans received higher star ratings (on average) in later years. The most common star rating in 2010 was 2.5, in 2012 it was 3, and in 2015 it was 4.

Question 4¶

=====================================================================
                               Dependent variable:                   
             --------------------------------------------------------
                                   Market Share                      
               2010      2011      2012      2013     2014     2015  
                (1)       (2)       (3)      (4)      (5)      (6)   
---------------------------------------------------------------------
3 Stars      0.013***  0.035***  0.031***  0.062*** 0.027*** 0.027***
              (0.002)   (0.002)   (0.003)  (0.003)  (0.006)  (0.005) 
                                                                     
3.5 Stars      0.001   0.019***  0.013***  0.038*** 0.027*** 0.039***
              (0.002)   (0.003)   (0.003)  (0.003)  (0.006)  (0.004) 
                                                                     
4 Stars       -0.004*    0.003   -0.025*** 0.017***  0.007   0.011***
              (0.002)   (0.003)   (0.004)  (0.003)  (0.006)  (0.004) 
                                                                     
4.5+ Stars   -0.008*** -0.009*** -0.013*** 0.022***  0.007   0.023***
              (0.003)   (0.003)   (0.003)  (0.003)  (0.006)  (0.004) 
                                                                     
Constant     0.063***  0.070***  0.079***  0.049*** 0.064*** 0.061***
              (0.001)   (0.002)   (0.003)  (0.003)  (0.006)  (0.004) 
                                                                     
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Observations  18,993    19,260    20,895    23,755   22,793   22,949 
R2             0.004     0.019     0.023    0.021    0.007    0.008  
Adjusted R2    0.004     0.019     0.023    0.021    0.006    0.008  
=====================================================================
Note:                                     *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01

In general, higher star ratings are associated with greater market share across years.

Estimate ATEs¶

A tibble: 5 × 2
Star RatingPlans Rounded Up
<dbl><int>
3.04425
3.54605
4.05518
4.51095
5.0 30

Question 6¶

A tibble: 6 × 3
Coefficient3 Stars3.5 Stars
<chr><dbl><dbl>
Intercept 0.016 0.102
Rounded 0.055 -0.040
Running Score -0.740 0.417
Rounded × Score 0.958 -0.467
Observations 5309.0001882.000
R-squared 0.059 0.015

Question 7¶

My results are somewhat sensitive to the choice of bandwidth, tending to increase slightly (on average) as the bandwidth increases. The effect of receiving a 3-star rating on enrollments is around 0.05, but increases to nearly 0.10 at higher bandwidths. The effect of receiving a 3.5-star rating on market share is negative for all bandwidth values, but increases slightly as bandwidth increases.

Question 8¶

The distrubution of the running variable does not seem to change meaningfully before vs. after the 3.0 and 3.5 thresholds. There is no clear spike immediately to the right of the thresholds, so there is not strong evidence that contracts manipulate the running variable.

Question 9¶

A tibble: 4 × 3
CutoffCharacteristicDiff
<chr><chr><dbl>
3 stars HMO 0.42716545
3 stars PartD 0.29107216
3.5 starsHMO -0.22885889
3.5 starsPartD-0.04690552

The table and love plot indicate that around 40% more plans are HMOs at the 3-star threshold vs 2.5-stars. Also, plans just above the 3.0 threshold are more likely to have Part D. In contrast, the differences for the 3.5 star threshold are negative but smaller in magnitude. Plans just above the threshold value for 3.5 stars are slightly less likely to be HMOs or to have Part D. This suggests some differences in HMO and Part D status near the 3-star threshold and slight evidence of differences at the 3.5-star threshold.

Question 10¶

Based on my analysis, increasing a star rating has a slight positive effect on enrollments at the 3-star threshold and a small negative effect at the 3.5-star threshold. The RD estimate at 3 stars is around 0.05 and statistically significant, while the estimate at 3.5 stars is around -0.04 and significant with a bandwidth of 0.125. This could be because customers perceive 3 stars as significantly better than 2.5 stars, while the jump from 3 to 3.5 stars might be seen as less important. The results are somewhat similar across bandwidths, though the effect of receiving 3 stars increases slightly as bandwidth increases. There is no clear evidence of manipulation of the running variable, though plans just above the 3-star threshold are more likely to be HMOs and to offer Part D coverage than plans just below the threshold.
